title: Box<T> — heap allocation
instruction:
`Box<T>` stores a value on the heap. The Box itself lives on the stack.

Run this.
successMessage: Box<T> owns the heap value. When Box is dropped, the heap memory is freed. *b dereferences to get the i32.

title: Recursive data with Box
instruction:
A recursive type needs Box to have a known size at compile time.

Run this.
successMessage: Without Box, Cons would be infinitely large. Box breaks the cycle: the Box itself has a known size (one pointer), and it points to the rest on the heap.

title: Rc<T> — shared ownership
instruction:
`Rc<T>` (reference counted) allows multiple owners. The data is freed when the last Rc drops.

Run this.
successMessage: Rc::clone does not deep-copy — it increments the reference count. The data is freed when count reaches 0. Single-threaded only.

title: RefCell<T> — interior mutability
instruction:
`RefCell<T>` allows mutable borrows at runtime even through shared references.

Run this.
successMessage: RefCell moves the borrow checks to runtime. If you violate the rules at runtime, it panics. Use when you know borrows are correct but the compiler can't verify it.

title: Rc<RefCell<T>> — shared mutable
instruction:
Combine `Rc` and `RefCell` for shared mutable data.

Run this.
successMessage: Rc<RefCell<T>> is the Rust way to have multiple references that can mutate shared data. Both a and b modified the same value.

title: Box and Deref
instruction:
Smart pointers implement `Deref` so you can use them like regular references.

Run this.
successMessage: Deref coercion: &Box<String> -> &String -> &str automatically. This chain makes Box<String> usable wherever &str is expected.

title: The Drop trait
instruction:
Implement `Drop` to run code when a value goes out of scope.

Run this.
successMessage: Drop runs in reverse order of creation (LIFO). This is how Rust implements RAII — resource cleanup is automatic and deterministic.

title: Weak<T> — avoid reference cycles
instruction:
`Weak<T>` holds a non-owning reference — it does not prevent the data from being freed.

Run this.
successMessage: Weak::upgrade() returns Option — Some if the data is still alive, None if it was freed. Use Weak to break reference cycles that would otherwise leak memory.
